title: Progressive waves
paperName: Paper 1: Multiple Choice / Paper 2: AS Level Structured Questions
summary: Master progressive waves for Cambridge 9702: amplitude, frequency, wavelength, speed, phase difference and the wave equation explained clearly.
learningObjectives: Define and apply the key wave quantities — amplitude, frequency, period, wavelength, and wave speed — using the wave equation v = fλ
Describe what a progressive wave is and explain how energy is transferred without net movement of the medium
Calculate phase difference between two points on a wave and express it correctly in degrees or radians

simpleExplanation.title: Waves in plain English
simpleExplanation.summary: A progressive wave transfers energy from one place to another through oscillations, but the medium itself does not travel with the wave. Think of it as a pattern of disturbance that moves through space. Every point on the wave oscillates about its rest position with the same frequency as the source.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine doing 'the wave' in a football stadium — each person stands up and sits down in turn, but no one actually moves along the rows. The wave pattern travels around the stadium while the people (the medium) simply bob up and down on the spot.
steps: Learn the six key quantities: amplitude (A), frequency (f), period (T), wavelength (λ), wave speed (v), and phase difference (φ).
Memorise the wave equation v = fλ and the relationship T = 1/f — these appear in almost every waves question.
Understand that amplitude determines energy: doubling the amplitude quadruples the intensity (I ∝ A²).
Practise calculating phase difference using φ = 2πx/λ (in radians) or φ = 360°× x/λ (in degrees), where x is the separation between two points.

sections.content: Progressive waves are the foundation of the entire waves section in Cambridge 9702. Get these definitions sharp and the rest — stationary waves, diffraction, superposition — will make far more sense. A **progressive wave** is a wave that transfers **energy** from one point to another through a medium (or through a vacuum, in the case of electromagnetic waves) by means of oscillations. The key distinction: the medium does not travel with the wave. Individual particles simply **oscillate about their equilibrium positions**. This topic is examined on both Paper 1 (single best answer) and Paper 2 (structured questions requiring definitions, calculations, and graph interpretation).
Key Wave Quantities — Definitions You Must Know

Cambridge mark schemes are precise — vague definitions lose marks. Learn these word-for-word:

• **Amplitude (A):** The maximum displacement of a particle from its equilibrium (rest) position. Unit: metre (m).

• **Period (T):** The time taken for one complete oscillation of a particle (or for one complete wave pattern to pass a fixed point). Unit: second (s).

• **Frequency (f):** The number of complete oscillations per unit time. Unit: hertz (Hz). Note: f = 1/T.

• **Wavelength (λ):** The minimum distance between two points on a wave that are **in phase** with each other (i.e. at the same stage of their oscillation). Unit: metre (m).

• **Wave speed (v):** The distance travelled by the wave pattern (the wavefront) per unit time. Unit: m s⁻¹.

• **Phase difference (φ):** A measure of how much one point on a wave leads or lags behind another point, expressed in degrees or radians.

v = fλ       where v = wave speed (m s⁻¹), f = frequency (Hz), λ = wavelength (m)

Also: T = 1/f       and       v = λ/T

The wave equation links the three most-tested quantities in a single relationship. It applies to **all** progressive waves — transverse and longitudinal, mechanical and electromagnetic. In a given medium, wave speed is determined by the properties of that medium (e.g. tension and density for a string, temperature for sound in air). If frequency changes — for example by changing the source — the wavelength adjusts to keep v constant.

**Transverse waves:** The oscillations of the medium are **perpendicular** to the direction of wave travel (energy transfer). Examples: electromagnetic waves (light, radio waves), waves on a string, water surface waves. These can be **polarised** because the oscillation has a defined plane.

**Longitudinal waves:** The oscillations of the medium are **parallel** to the direction of wave travel. Examples: sound waves, ultrasound, compression waves in a slinky. They consist of alternating **compressions** (regions of higher pressure/density) and **rarefactions** (regions of lower pressure/density). Longitudinal waves **cannot** be polarised.

A common Paper 1 trap: water waves look like they travel horizontally, but the surface particles actually move in circles (or ellipses), not purely up-and-down — be precise if asked.

**Phase** describes the stage of oscillation a particle has reached in its cycle at a given instant. Two points on a wave are said to be:

• **In phase** if their phase difference is 0, 2π, 4π rad (or 0°, 360°, 720°…) — they are separated by a whole number of wavelengths.

• **In antiphase** (completely out of phase) if their phase difference is π, 3π rad (or 180°, 540°…) — they are separated by an odd number of half-wavelengths.

Phase difference between two points separated by a distance x along the wave:

φ = (2π / λ) × x       [result in radians]

or equivalently:

φ = (360° / λ) × x       [result in degrees]

A progressive wave transfers energy in the direction of wave travel. The rate of energy transfer per unit area perpendicular to the wave is called **intensity (I)**, measured in W m⁻².

For a point source radiating uniformly in all directions (e.g. a lamp or a sound source in open air), the intensity follows an inverse square law:

**I ∝ 1/r²**  (where r is the distance from the source)

Critically, intensity is also proportional to the **square of the amplitude**:

**I ∝ A²**

So if amplitude doubles, intensity quadruples. If intensity halves, amplitude decreases by a factor of √2. This relationship is regularly tested in Paper 2 calculations.
I ∝ A²       and       I = P / (4πr²)  for a point source

items: A progressive wave transfers energy without net transfer of matter — particles oscillate about fixed equilibrium positions.
Always quote the definition of wavelength as the distance between two adjacent points that are **in phase** (not simply 'the length of one wave').
The wave equation v = fλ applies to all wave types; rearrange carefully to find whichever quantity is unknown.
Phase difference in radians = (2π/λ) × separation; one full wavelength of separation = 2π rad = 360° = in phase.
Intensity is proportional to amplitude squared (I ∝ A²); this is the correct way to link wave energy to amplitude in 9702.
Transverse waves can be polarised; longitudinal waves cannot — this is a quick one-mark discriminator in Paper 1.

sections.question:
A sound wave travels through air at a speed of 340 m s⁻¹ with a frequency of 850 Hz.

(a) Calculate the wavelength of the sound wave.
(b) Two points P and Q lie along the direction of travel of the wave. Point Q is 0.30 m further from the source than point P. Calculate the phase difference between P and Q, expressing your answer in radians.
(c) State whether P and Q are in phase, in antiphase, or neither.
sections.solution:
**Part (a) — Finding wavelength:**

Using v = fλ, rearrange to give λ = v/f

λ = 340 / 850
λ = 0.40 m  ✓

**Part (b) — Phase difference:**

φ = (2π / λ) × x
φ = (2π / 0.40) × 0.30
φ = (2π × 0.30) / 0.40
φ = 0.60π / 0.40  [careful here — let's compute directly]
φ = 2π × 0.75
φ = 1.5π rad  =  3π/2 rad  ≈  4.71 rad  ✓

**Part (c) — Phase relationship:**

The separation x = 0.30 m = 0.75λ (three-quarters of a wavelength).
Since 0.75λ is neither a whole number nor a half-integer number of wavelengths, P and Q are **neither in phase nor in antiphase**.

(In antiphase they would need to be separated by 0.20 m, 0.60 m, 1.00 m…)

sections.content: When defining 'wavelength' in a Paper 2 question, never just write 'the length of one wave' — that scores zero. You must say: 'the minimum distance between two points **oscillating in phase**' or equivalently 'the distance between two adjacent points at the same stage of their oscillation.' Cambridge mark schemes look for the key phrases 'minimum distance' and 'in phase.' Practise writing these definitions in full under timed conditions.

In Paper 1, watch out for questions that give you a displacement–distance graph AND a displacement–time graph for the same wave. The distance graph gives you λ directly. The time graph gives you T (and hence f). Both together let you calculate v = fλ. A very common distractor is mixing up the x-axis: one is position along the wave, the other is time — they look identical in shape.

sections.label: Mark a past-paper question on this topic

faq.q: What are the key definitions I need to know for progressive waves in Cambridge 9702?
faq.a: For 9702, you must be able to define amplitude, period, frequency, wavelength, wave speed, and phase difference precisely. The most commonly dropped marks come from vague definitions — for example, defining wavelength as 'the length of one wave' rather than 'the minimum distance between two points oscillating in phase.' Our free course walks you through examiner-approved wording for every definition, with past paper practice built in.

faq.q: How do I calculate phase difference between two points on a progressive wave?
faq.a: Use the formula φ = (2π / λ) × x, where x is the distance between the two points along the direction of wave travel and λ is the wavelength. This gives the phase difference in radians. If you need degrees, use φ = (360° / λ) × x instead. A separation of one full wavelength gives φ = 2π rad (in phase); a separation of half a wavelength gives φ = π rad (in antiphase). Try applying this with our free past paper question sets for topic 7.1.

faq.q: What is the difference between transverse and longitudinal waves, and why does it matter for 9702?
faq.a: In a transverse wave, particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of energy transfer (e.g. light, waves on a string). In a longitudinal wave, particles oscillate parallel to the direction of energy transfer (e.g. sound). The key exam implication is polarisation: only transverse waves can be polarised — this is a reliable one-mark discriminator in Paper 1. Our 9702 revision lessons cover both wave types with worked examples and multiple-choice strategies.
